coredata.dc:title: Turn-off fluorescence sensor for the detection of ferric ion in water using green synthesized N-doped carbon dots and its bio-imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Atchudan, Raji
Shim, Jae-Jin
Kalimuthu, Senthilkumar
Ahn, Byeong-Cheol
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: This paper reports turn-off fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ ion in water using fluorescent N-doped carbon dots as a probe. A simple and efficient hydrothermal carbonization of Prunus avium fruit extract for the synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) is described. This green approach proceeds quickly and provides good quality N-CDs. The mean size of synthesized N-CDs was approximately 7nm calculated from the high-resolution transmission electron microscopic images. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy revealed the presence of –OH, –NH2, –COOH, and –CO functional groups over the surface of CDs. The N-CDs showed excellent fluorescent properties, and emitted blue fluorescence at 411nm upon excitation at 310nm. The calculated quantum yield of the synthesized N-CDs is 13% against quinine sulfate as a reference fluorophore. The synthesized N-CDs were used as a fluorescent probe towards the selective and sensitive detection of biologically important Fe3+ ions in water by fluorescence spectroscopy and for bio-imaging of MDA-MB-231 cells. The limit of detection (LOD) and the Stern–Volmer quenching constant for the synthesized N-CDs were 0.96μM and 2.0958×103 M of Fe3+ ions. The green synthesized N-CDs are efficiently used as a promising candidate for the detection of Fe3+ ions and bio-imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Prunus avium
N-doped carbon dot
Fluorescence
Fe3+ sensor
Cytotoxicity
Bio-imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) Cell Viability Assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-c) HRTEM images and (d) size distribution of the synthesized N-CDs.
(a) HAADF-STEM image, elemental mapping (b) carbon, (c) nitrogen, (d) oxygen, and (e) EDS of synthesized N-CDs.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectrum of synthesized N-CDs.
FT-IR spectra of the P. avium fruit extract and the synthesized N-CDs.
XPS survey-scan spectrum (a), high resolution XP spectrum of carbon (b), nitrogen (c) and oxygen (d) of synthesized N-CDs.
(a) Excitation and emission spectrum of the synthesized N-CDs (ex: 310nm) and (b) fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs at various excitation wavelengths.
The plausible formation mechanism of N-CDs from the major phytoconstituents of P. avium.
Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (from 0 to 1000μM) in aqueous solution.
Plausible mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ ions.
Fluorescence microscopic images of MDA-MB-231 cells in the absence and presence of N-CDs (20 and 40μL) at 1h incubation.

head.ce:title: Turn-off fluorescence sensor for the detection of ferric ion in water using green synthesized N-doped carbon dots and its bio-imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 712-749, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nuclear Medicine, Kyungpook National University School of Medicine, Daegu 700-721, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Kyungpook National University School of Medicine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports turn-off fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ ion in water using fluorescent N-doped carbon dots as a probe. A simple and efficient hydrothermal carbonization of Prunus avium fruit extract for the synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) is described. This green approach proceeds quickly and provides good quality N-CDs. The mean size of synthesized N-CDs was approximately 7nm calculated from the high-resolution transmission electron microscopic images. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy revealed the presence of –OH, –NH2, –COOH, and –CO functional groups over the surface of CDs. The N-CDs showed excellent fluorescent properties, and emitted blue fluorescence at 411nm upon excitation at 310nm. The calculated quantum yield of the synthesized N-CDs is 13% against quinine sulfate as a reference fluorophore. The synthesized N-CDs were used as a fluorescent probe towards the selective and sensitive detection of biologically important Fe3+ ions in water by fluorescence spectroscopy and for bio-imaging of MDA-MB-231 cells. The limit of detection (LOD) and the Stern–Volmer quenching constant for the synthesized N-CDs were 0.96μM and 2.0958×103 M of Fe3+ ions. The green synthesized N-CDs are efficiently used as a promising candidate for the detection of Fe3+ ions and bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a promising material for sensors, energy and bio-imaging owing to their attractive optical, catalytic and electronic properties [1–3]. CDs are a new class of the carbon family with sizes ranging below 10nm, which is a better replacement for heavy metal-based quantum dots because of its low cytotoxicity, hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, and non-blinking emission [4,5]. Moreover, it has been suggested that CDs have up-converting properties, i.e., fluorescence and excitation dependent emission, which makes them applicable to live cell imaging, flow cytometry and sensor fields [6]. Therefore, the emission wavelength of CDs can be adjusted from the UV region to the near-infrared region by changing the excitation wavelength according to the requirements. The formation and mechanism of fluorescence of CDs are still unclear. The fluorescent quantum yields (intensity) of CDs depend strongly on the carbon precursor, surface defects, doping agent, solvent, and size [7,8]. In general, CDs have been synthesized by a number of physical, chemical and electrochemical methods, such as laser ablation of graphite, hydrothermal and solvo-thermal carbonization of simple organic compounds, microwave pyrolysis, and ultra-sonication [9,10]. Some of these techniques require costly equipment, toxic starting materials and involve tedious processes, which severely affect the yield and properties of CDs [11]. The doping of heteroatoms on CDs provides improved fluorescence quantum yields and high catalytic activity compared to that of un-doped CDs. Therefore, the preparation of heteroatom-doped CDs becomes significant for bio-imaging and sensing applications [12]. Currently, researchers are focusing on the synthesis of highly fluorescent CDs by a simple and green way. Several green approaches for the preparation of CDs have been reported, including the use of inexpensive plant extracts, fruit juices, hair, milk, and edible waste as the carbon precursors [13–18].

Prunus avium (P. avium) belongs to the Rosaceae family, and is native to Europe. The species is commonly called wild cherry, and is cultivated mainly in Japan, Turkey, Korea, and China. P. avium is one of the commercially important fruit crops in the world, and it is a good source of phenolic compounds, sugars and organic acids. Kelebek and Selli evaluated the chemical constituents of P. avium fruit and identified four sugars (sucrose, glucose, fructose, and sorbitol), four organic acids (malic, citric, shikimic, and fumaric acid) and eleven phenolic compounds, including hydroxycinnamic acids, anthocyanins, flavan-3-ols and flavonol [19]. In this regard, P. avium fruits were selected as a carbon source for the synthesis of CDs.

Iron is one of the most important metal ions in the metabolism, and involved in a variety of cell functions, making it essential for the proper functioning of all living cells [20]. In particular, the ferric ion (Fe3+) commonly exists in many proteins and enzymes. On the other hand, iron is harmful when present in excess, which creates serious complications, such as like β-thalassemia, hereditary hemochromatosis and Alzheimer's disease. Therefore, the detection and sensing of Fe3+ is important for the environment and human health [21,22]. MDA-MB-231 is an estrogen-negative human breast cancer cell line which was established on 1973 from a sample of pleural effusion obtained from a white woman. [23]

This paper reports the facile one-step synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) by the hydrothermal treatment of P. avium fruit extract as the carbon precursor and aqueous ammonia as the nitrogen dopant. The synthesized N-CDs were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) with elemental mapping, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The N-CDs were applied as a fluorescent probe towards the sensing of Fe3+ ion in water and also as a staining probe in the bio-imaging of MDA-MB-231 cells.

The P. avium fruits were obtained from Yeungnam University Campus, Republic of Korea. An aqueous ammonia solution (25%), chloride salts of Cu2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, and Al3+, lead nitrate (Pb2+), and cadmium acetate (Cd2+) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout this study. For the sensor studies, all the solutions of metal ions were prepared in DI water.

The P. avium fruits were washed thoroughly with DI water and then crushed without the seed. The crushed extract was filtered through cotton followed by Whatman filter paper. For the synthesis of N-CDs, 2mL of aqueous ammonia (N-dopant) was mixed with 23mL of the filtered fruit extract (around pH9) and transferred in to a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave (Capacity of 50mL). The autoclave was sealed carefully and subsequently heated to 180°C for 5h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The resulting dark brown solution was centrifuged, the precipitate was discarded and the supernatant liquid was subjected to analysis. Fig. S1 (see Supporting information) presents a schematic diagram of the synthesis of N-CDs.

For HRTEM analysis, the sample was prepared by placing one drop of the N-CDs on a carbon-coated copper grid. After drying, the morphology of the N-CDs was investigated using FEI-Tecnai TF-20 operated at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. Simultaneously, EDS and elemental mapping were also conducted using the same instrument.

The colloidal N-CDs were drop coated onto a new glass plate. After drying, the wide-angle XRD patterns were obtained from a PANalytical X'Pert 3 MRD diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54Å, 40kV and 30mA). The XRD patterns were recorded over the 2θ range, 10–90°, and at a step interval of 0.02° with a counting time of 5s per point.
The Raman spectra of the N-CDs were recorded using a Thermo Scientific DXR SmartRaman spectrometer with a 532nm excitation.

The FT-IR (PerkinElmer Spectrum Two) spectra of the P. avium fruit extract and synthesized N-CDs were obtained using the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory. The surface chemical analysis of N-CDs was performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific). The obtained raw XPS spectra have been deconvoluted using CasaXPS processing software.

The optical properties of the synthesized N-CDs were studied using an OPTIZEN 3220UV UV–Visible spectrophotometer and Hitachi-7000 F fluorescence spectrometer. To determine the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of the N-CDs, the excitation wavelength was varied from a lower to higher wavelength (280 to 360nm). The slit width was fixed to 5nm and the scan speed was set to 240nm/min. The fluorescence quantum yield of the synthesized N-CDs was calculated according to previous report [24].

30μL of the synthesized N-CDs were taken in a 20mL standard measuring flask and made up to the mark using a phosphate buffer solution (pH7), which was used as the fluorescent probe throughout the sensor studies. For fluorescence sensing, 0.5mL of the probe was mixed with 0.5mL of various concentrations of metal cations from 0.1 mM and 1.0 mM stock solutions. The fluorescence (Hitachi-7000 F) measurements were carried out after 5 min incubation at room temperature using an excitation wavelength of 310nm with a slit width of 5nm and a scan speed of 240nm/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) Cell Viability Assay
ce:section.ce:para:
The MDA-MB-231 cells were procured from Korean cell line bank, Seoul. The CCK-8 cytotoxicity assay was performed on the MDA-MB-231 cells with various concentrations of N-CDs. The MDA-MB-231 cells were seeded in a 96 well plate at a density of 5×103 cells per well. The synthesized N-CDs at various concentrations, ranging from 0 to 40μL (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35 and 40μL), were added to the wells and incubated for 24h for culture (the medium contained a 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin–streptomycin mixture). The cells were grown in a CO2 incubator at 37°C and 5% CO2. After 24h, 10μL of the CCK-8 solution (Dojindo laboratories, Kumamoto, Japan) was added to each well and incubated for 1h. After incubation, the absorbance was measured at 450nm using a microplate reader (Bio-rad). The % cell viability was calculated using the following Eq. (1).
(1)
Cell
viability
%
=
Abs
s
Abs
c
× 100
where Abs(s) and Abs(c) are the absorbance of sample and absorbance of control, respectively.

The MDA-MB-231 cells were cultured with 20 and 40μL of the N-CDs and without N-CDs (blank) in a growth medium supplemented with a 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin–streptomycin mixture. The cells were grown in a CO2 incubator at 37°C and 5% CO2 for 1h. After incubation, the medium was removed and the cells were washed with PBS. The N-CDs incubated cells were analyzed by fluorescence microscopy (Nikon Eclipse Ti) under ultraviolet excitation.

The fluorescent N-CDs were prepared by the hydrothermal carbonization of P. avium (pH9) fruit extract using a simple and green approach. After 5h digestion at 180°C, the color of the fruit extract of P. avium was changed to a blackish brown, indicating the formation of carbon dots [25].

Fig. 1 presents HRTEM images of the N-CDs at different scale ranges and the size distribution. The synthesized N-CDs were near spherical in shape and distributed uniformly over the carbon disc. The average size of the synthesized N-CDs was 7nm. In the high resolution images, lattice fringes were clearly observed and the calculated d-spacing was approximately 0.22nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [26]. Fig. 2 shows the high angle annular dark field (HAADF) scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) images, energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) and elemental maps of the synthesized N-CDs; the N-CDs consisted mainly of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen.

Fig. 3 presents the XRD pattern and Raman spectrum of the N-CDs. The XRD pattern shows two broad peaks at 24° and 42° 2θ, which were assigned to the (002) and (100) planes of graphitic carbon [27,28]. The d-spacing of the N-CDs was calculated using Bragg's Eq. (2) based on the 2θ position of the (002) plane.
(2)
nλ = 2 dsin θ
or
d =
nλ
2 sin θ
where n is a positive integer (1), λ is the wavelength of incident X-rays (λ=1.54Å) and θ is the position of the (002) plane. The calculated d-spacing of the N-CDs was approximately 0.37nm and 0.215nm for the (002) and (100) planes, respectively, which is consistent with the HRTEM results. In addition, the d-spacing value of (002) plane was slightly higher than normal graphene sheets which might be due to clear separation of graphitic carbon sheets. Moreover, the (002) diffraction plane was broader because of the formation of small sized N-CDs [29]. The XRD results suggest that the obtained N-CDs were moderately graphitic in nature.

The Raman spectrum of N-CDs revealed two different vibrations at approximately 1560 and 1360cm−1, corresponding to the graphitic (G) band of sp2 carbon atoms and disordered (D) graphite band of carbon atoms with dangling bonds [30,31]. The peak intensities of the D-band and G-band are denoted as ID and IG, respectively. The ratio of the intensity (ID/IG) was 0.78, which revealed the presence of defective carbon structures in the synthesized N-CDs. This may explain the fluorescent properties of the synthesized N-CDs [32]. Therefore, the Raman results suggest that the synthesized N-CDs have a moderate graphitic structure with minor surface defects.

Fig. 4 presents the FT-IR spectra of the P. avium fruit extract and the synthesized N-CDs. The FT-IR spectrum of P. avium fruit extract shows the major absorption bands at 3306, 2931 and 1717cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching of phenolic –OH, –CH and –CO (carboxylic acid) functional groups present in the extract [33]. In addition, three bands also appeared at 1600, 1408 and 1028cm−1, which were assigned to the bending vibrations of –OH, the –CH moiety (deformation) and –C–O stretching of the spiro moiety present in the phytoconstituents of P. avium. In the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs, the band at 3200–3400cm−1 was broadened, which may be due to overlap of the –OH/–NH stretching bands [34]. Interestingly, a sharp band was observed at 1603cm−1, which was assigned to the stretching of the CC moiety present in the core of the N-CDs. Moreover, the absorption bands that appear in the fruit extract of P. avium were also observed in the FT-IR of N-CDs with slight changes. This suggests that the phytoconstituents of P. avium covered the surface of the N-CDs, which lead to an improvement of the aqueous solubility of N-CDs for potential applications in the bio-imaging and sensor fields.

Fig. 5 shows the XPS survey scan spectrum of N-CDs and its high resolution XP spectra. The survey scan spectrum of the N-CDs consists of three major binding energy peaks at approximately 285, 400 and 532eV, which corresponds to the binding peaks of C (1s), N (1s) and O (1s), respectively. The high resolution XP spectrum of C (1s) was de-convoluted into three major binding peaks at 284.3, 285.3, and 286.6eV, which were assigned to the CC/C-C, C-N/C-O and CO functionalities over the surface of the CDs [35,36]. The N (1s) spectrum of N-CDs contained one major peak at 399.7eV, which indicated the presence of the N–C (H2N–C) moiety on the surface of the CDs [37]. The high resolution spectrum of O (1s) was de-convoluted into two binding peaks at 530.5 and 532.2eV, due to the existence of OC and HO–C/O–C groups [38]. Therefore, the surface analysis results suggested that the CDs had been functionalized by hydroxyl (–C–OH), amine (C–NH2) and carboxyl (–CO) groups.

Fig. S2 (see Supporting information) presents the UV–Vis spectrum of the N-CDs, which showed two major absorption peaks at 270 and 332nm, corresponding to the π–π* transition of CC (sp2) bonds and the n–π* transition of CO (carbonyl groups). The carboxyl groups were located on the CDs surface and CC groups were available in the core of the CDs [39]. Fig. 6 (a) shows the excitation and emission spectra of the synthesized N-CDs.

The synthesized N-CDs emitted blue fluorescence with excitation at 365nm using a table top UV light. The fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs were investigated under different excitation wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 6 (b). The emission intensity and emission wavelengths of the N-CDs depend strongly on the excitation wavelengths from 280 to 360nm. Initially, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs was lower at 280nm excitation. The intensity was increased gradually with increasing excitation wavelength from 280 to 310nm. In addition, the intensity decreased with increasing excitation wavelength from 320nm to 360nm and the emission wavelengths were shifted to a higher wavelength. The synthesized N-CDs showed the maximum fluorescence intensity at 411nm with an excitation wavelength of 310nm. The excitation-dependent emission property of CDs is common and was attributed to the different sizes of the CDs and the distribution of different surface states due to the various organic functional groups (–OH, –NH2 and –COOH) on the surface of CDs [40–42]. The calculated quantum yield of the synthesized N-CDs is 13% against quinine sulfate as reference and this value is higher than that of the some reported CDs [17]. The high quantum yield is probably due to the doping of nitrogen and its functional groups.

The major phytoconstituents present in the fruits of P. avium are glucose and malic acid which are taken in to account for the formation of CDs [19]. The formation of CDs by the hydrothermal carbonization of glucose and malic acid is complicated. The plausible formation mechanism of N-CDs was proposed and presented in Fig. 7 , which involves dehydration, polymerization, carbonization/aromatization, and passivation. Initially, the nitrogen dopant (ammonia) might be reacts with carbonyl groups of glucose and malic acid which forms stable ammonium salts. At 100°C, the hydroxyl groups undergo dehydration and converted to furfural derivatives. Further, the polymerization and condensation of furfural derivatives yielded water soluble polymers, which further undergoes aromatization/carbonization via aldol condensation, cycloaddition and furan resin condensation and finally gives passivated N-CDs [43,44].

XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy revealed –OH, –COOH and –NH2 functionalities over the surface of the synthesized N-CDs, which comes from the phytoconstituents of P. avium fruit extract. The coordination ability of these functional groups allowed a study of the sensing behavior of the synthesized N-CDs towards metal cations in water. The green synthesized and functionalized CDs were applied successfully as a fluorescent quenching probe towards the sensing of metal cations, anions and toxic chemicals [45–48]. The selectivity of the synthesized N-CDs were tested by the addition of aqueous 250μM solutions of various metal ions, such as Cu2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Pb2+, and Cd2+. Fig. S3 (see Supporting information) shows the corresponding fluorescence spectra and bar diagram of the N-CDs with and without (blank) metal ions. The figure showed that Fe3+ had more tendency to quench the fluorescence of the N-CDs compared to all other metal ions studied. After the addition of 250μM of Fe3+ to the N-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs decreased approximately 200 folds, which indicates the high selectivity of synthesized N-CDs towards the detection of Fe3+ owing to the stronger affinity of Fe3+ than the other metal ions.

The sensitivity of the synthesized N-CDs towards the sensing of Fe3+ ions were analyzed by fluorescence titrations. Fig. 8 shows the fluorescence titration spectra of the N-CDs with various concentrations of Fe3+. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs was quenched gradually while increasing the concentrations of Fe3+ ions from 0 to 1000μM. The quenched intensity was linearly correlated with the concentrations of Fe3+ from 0 to 100μM, as shown in Fig. S4 (a) (see Supporting information).

The calculated linear coefficient (R2) between the fluorescence intensity and various concentrations of Fe3+ ions was 0.9869. This suggests that the synthesized N-CDs were highly sensitive towards Fe3+ [49,50]. Moreover, the quenching efficiency of the Fe3+ ions was analyzed using the Stern–Volmer relation (3).
(3)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
Q
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions, respectively, Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the concentration of Fe3+ ions.

The limit of detection (LOD) of the synthesized N-CDs on ferric ion in water was calculated using the following formula (4) [51]:
(4)
LOD =
3 S
b
where S is the standard deviation of (F0/F) values and b is the slope of the linear line.

Fig. S4 (b) (see Supporting information) presents the F0/F Vs [Fe3+] graph, the figure suggests that the concentrations of Fe3+ (0–100μM) and the corresponding F0/F values were linearly correlated (R2 =0.9880) with an intercept of 0.98435. The Stern–Volmer quenching constant can be obtained by rearranging the relation according to Eq. (2). The calculated quenching constant was 2.0958×103 M. The LOD was estimated to be 0.96μM, which is a very low concentration [52]. These results suggest that the synthesized N-CDs can sensitively detect even very low concentrations (0.96μM) of Fe3+ ions in water by fluorescence quenching.

Fig. 9 presents the proposed mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+. A phenolic hydroxyl group will form a complex with ferric ions. FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS confirmed the presence of abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups over the surface of the N-CDs. After the addition of Fe3+ ions to the fluorescent N-CDs, the phenolic hydroxyl functionalities formed a stable ground state iron-phenol complex. Therefore, non-radiative electron or energy transfer is possible from the N-CDs surface, resulting in concomitant fluorescence quenching [53].

A cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) cytotoxicity assay was performed at different concentrations of synthesized N-CDs with MDA-MB-231 cells, and the results are shown in Fig. S5 (see Supporting information).

The results showed no considerable changes (97%) in cell viability, even in the presence of high concentrations of N-CDs (40μL), which suggests that the synthesized N-CDs has very low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility against MDA-MB-231 cells [54,55]. The N-CDs were used further for the fluorescence bio-imaging of MDA-MB-231 cells, as depicted in Fig. 10 . The N-CDs treated cells displayed blue emission compared to the blank with excitation in the UV region. In addition, 40μL of the N-CDs contained cells glowing with high fluorescence compared to 20μL of N-CDs, which may be due to the difference in concentration and the penetration of N-CDs into the cell membranes of the MDA-MB-231 cells.

An eco-friendly, low-cost and facile method for the synthesis of N-CDs was developed using P. avium fruit extract as a carbon precursor and aqueous ammonia as the nitrogen dopant. The synthesized N-CDs were characterized by HR-TEM, XRD, Raman, FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS. The phytoconstituents (phenolic –OH, carboxyl and carbonyl) of the P. avium fruit present in the surface of N-CDs was identified by XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy. The synthesized N-CDs emitted blue fluorescence at 411nm with excitation at 310nm. The synthesized N-CDs can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for the selective and sensitive turn-off detection of Fe3+ ions in water. The LOD and the Stern–Volmer quenching constant for the synthesized N-CDs were 0.96μM and 2.0958×103 M of Fe3+ ions. The synthesized N-CDs showed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility towards MDA-MB-231 cells. These N-CDs can be used as a staining probe for the fluorescence imaging of MDA-MB-231 cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Yeungnam University Research Grant (214A345036).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2016.03.010.
